--- SOURCE 3: What is the significance of stakeholder engagement in Legal ... ---
URL: https://sbnsoftware.com/blog/what-is-the-significance-of-stakeholder-engagement-in-legal-registers/

SUMMARY:
Effective communication strategies for engagement are essential in facilitating meaningful dialogue and collaboration among stakeholders,

FULL CONTENT:
###### [1-844-5-ASK-SBN](tel:1-844-527-5726)  |  [1-844-527-5726](tel:1-844-527-5726)

![sbn-logo_green-1](https://sbnsoftware.com/wp-content/uploads/sbn-logo_green-1-1.svg)
![sbn-logo_green-1](https://sbnsoftware.com/wp-content/uploads/sbn-logo_green-1-1.svg)

# What is the significance of stakeholder engagement in Legal Registers?

![How can you analyze audit trail data for insights into EHS performance?](https://sbnsoftware.com/wp-content/uploads/PastedGraphic-1-930x279.png)

\*\*Introduction\*\*

In an increasingly complex and regulated business environment, stakeholder engagement plays a pivotal role in the development and maintenance of legal registers. A legal register serves as a comprehensive document that catalogs the relevant laws, regulations, and compliance obligations pertinent to an organization’s operations. It not only serves as a tool for ensuring legal conformity but also acts as a foundation for risk management and strategic decision-making. However, the effectiveness of a legal register hinges significantly on the degree of stakeholder engagement throughout its creation and ongoing management.

Stakeholder engagement in the context of legal registers involves collaborating with various individuals or groups who have an interest in or are impacted by an organization’s legal obligations. This includes employees at different levels, legal experts, compliance officers, industry regulators, and even external stakeholders like customers and community representatives. Engaging these stakeholders provides a wealth of perspectives that can enrich the legal register’s content, ensuring that it is not only comprehensive but also reflective of the realities on the ground. Furthermore, fostering active participation among stakeholders can enhance their understanding and commitment to legal compliance, leading to a culture of accountability and ethical practice within the organization.

The significance of stakeholder engagement in legal registers extends far beyond mere compliance. It promotes transparency, which is crucial for building trust among stakeholders and can serve as a competitive advantage in today’s market. By integrating diverse viewpoints, organizations can uncover potential risks and opportunities that may not be evident when relying solely on internal compliance teams. Additionally, stakeholder engagement can facilitate smoother implementation of legal requirements, as stakeholders who are actively involved are likelier to embrace necessary changes and contribute to creating a compliant and resilient organization. As businesses navigate the intricacies of legal landscapes, understanding and embracing the significance of stakeholder engagement in legal registers has become indispensable for fostering both compliance and sustainable organizational success.

## Stakeholder Identification and Mapping

Stakeholder identification and mapping is a crucial process in various fields, especially in legal and compliance contexts. It involves recognizing all parties that have an interest in or are affected by a particular issue or decision. This process is the foundation for effective stakeholder engagement and is essential for creating a comprehensive legal register. A legal register typically contains information about compliance obligations, risks, and relevant stakeholders who are impacted by these legal issues. By identifying and mapping stakeholders, organizations can ensure that they consider diverse perspectives and interests, ultimately leading to more inclusive and effective decision-making.

The significance of stakeholder engagement with a focus on legal registers lies in the fact that stakeholders often hold critical information and insights about compliance issues, regulatory changes, and potential risks. Engaging stakeholders enhances the quality and relevance of the legal register, as it incorporates input from those who might be directly affected by or knowledgeable about compliance matters. For instance, employees might provide insights into internal policies, while customers or suppliers could share their perspectives on how legal requirements impact their operations. When organizations actively involve stakeholders in the identification process, they can develop a legal register that is not only comprehensive but also aligned with the realities of the operational environment.

Moreover, mapping stakeholders allows organizations to prioritize their engagement strategies based on the influence and interest of each stakeholder group. This prioritization is vital, as it helps organizations to allocate resources effectively and develop targeted communication strategies that resonate with each group’s specific needs and concerns. Understanding the dynamics between various stakeholders can uncover potential areas of conflict or collaboration, ultimately fostering a proactive approach to compliance and risk management. In a rapidly changing regulatory landscape, stakeholder identification and mapping serve as essential tools for organizations seeking not only to meet their legal obligations but also to enhance their overall governance and accountability frameworks.

In conclusion, effective stakeholder identification and mapping are foundational to the success of legal registers. By fostering engagement with all relevant parties, organizations can ensure that their legal frameworks reflect a comprehensive understanding of compliance obligations and risks. This process not only enhances the legal registers themselves but also promotes a culture of collaboration, transparency, and proactive risk management.

## Communication Strategies for Engagement

Effective communication strategies for engagement are essential in facilitating meaningful dialogue and collaboration among stakeholders, especially in areas where legal compliance and regulatory frameworks are concerned. These strategies involve identifying appropriate channels and methods for disseminating information, gathering feedback, and fostering relationships with stakeholders. For organizations, particularly those operating in regulated industries, it is vital to recognize that stakeholders—including employees, customers, suppliers, regulatory bodies, and the community at large—have varying interests and levels of influence.

To develop robust communication strategies, organizations must first assess the specific needs and preferences of their stakeholder groups. Tailoring the communication approach to suit different audiences can enhance understanding and engagement. For instance, some stakeholders may prefer formal reports and newsletters, while others might find interactive workshops or digital platforms more engaging. Additionally, transparency in communication builds trust and demonstrates an organization’s commitment to involving stakeholders in decision-making processes. Implementing feedback mechanisms allows stakeholders to voice their concerns and suggestions, ensuring that their perspectives are considered in the organization’s operations.

The significance of stakeholder engagement, particularly in the context of legal registers, cannot be overstated. Legal registers are systematic compilations of regulations and compliance obligations that organizations must adhere to, and involving stakeholders in the development and maintenance of these registers enhances their relevance and effectiveness. By engaging stakeholders, organizations can gain valuable insights into the practical implications of legal requirements and how they impact various aspects of operations. This collaborative approach not only aids in accurate mapping of obligations but also helps identify areas of ambiguity in legal texts, allowing for proactive measures to address compliance challenges.

Ultimately, stakeholder engagement through well-crafted communication strategies contributes to a culture of compliance and accountability. When stakeholders are actively involved in discussions surrounding legal compliance, they tend to be more informed and invested in ensuring that operations align with legal requirements. This alignment reduces the risk of non-compliance and fosters an environment where legal and ethical standards are prioritized, leading to better overall governance and organizational performance. Engaging stakeholders in the conversation surrounding legal registers thus emerges as a crucial factor in enhancing the effectiveness and sustainability of an organization’s legal compliance efforts.

## Impact on Compliance and Risk Management

The impact of stakeholder engagement on compliance and risk management is profound and multifaceted. Engaging stakeholders, which includes employees, customers, suppliers, regulators, and community members, creates a platform for open dialogue about the various risks an organization may face and the compliance obligations it must fulfill. By involving stakeholders in the development and assessment of legal registers—the systematic collections of legal requirements pertinent to an organization—companies benefit from a more comprehensive understanding of their legal landscape and the associated risks.

When stakeholders are actively engaged, there is a greater likelihood that an organization will identify potential legal and regulatory risks early on. This proactive approach enables organizations to implement strategies to mitigate these risks before they escalate into serious compliance breaches or legal liabilities. For instance, feedback from operational staff can illuminate compliance challenges that leadership might overlook. Regular interactions with stakeholders can also reveal emerging risks related to changes in legislation or public sentiment, allowing organizations to adapt swiftly and remain compliant.

Moreover, stakeholder engagement fosters a culture of accountability and responsibility. When stakeholders understand their roles within the context of compliance, they are more likely to act in accordance with organizational standards and legal requirements. This collective awareness not only helps in managing risks but also builds a sense of shared responsibility for maintaining compliance. Furthermore, ample evidence shows that companies that prioritize stakeholder engagement often have stronger compliance records and are better positioned to navigate the complexities of legal frameworks.

In conclusion, the significance of stakeholder engagement in legal registers cannot be overstated. By actively involving stakeholders, organizations enhance their ability to manage compliance effectively and mitigate risks. This collaboration not only helps organizations to meet their legal obligations but also contributes to their overall resilience and sustainability in an ever-evolving regulatory environment.

## Stakeholder Influence on Policy Development

Stakeholder influence on policy development is a critical aspect of the policy-making process, particularly in contexts where laws and regulations are established. Stakeholders, which can include individuals, community groups, businesses, non-governmental organizations, and governmental entities, play a vital role in shaping policies that affect their interests and the broader society. Understanding their perspectives, needs, and concerns provides essential insights that help policymakers create more effective and inclusive legislation.

Engaging stakeholders throughout the policy development process is significant for several reasons. First, it ensures that diverse viewpoints are considered, which can lead to more balanced and equitable outcomes. When stakeholders have the opportunity to contribute their knowledge and experience, the policies developed are more likely to reflect the realities faced by those affected. This participatory approach can enhance the legitimacy of policies and foster greater public trust in the governing bodies responsible for creating and implementing them.

Moreover, stakeholder engagement can significantly impact compliance and adherence to policies. When stakeholders feel involved and their voices are heard in the policy-making process, they are more likely to support the resulting policies, leading to higher levels of compliance. The input from stakeholders can also help identify potential barriers to implementation early in the process, enabling policymakers to address these obstacles proactively. Consequently, effective stakeholder engagement can improve the chances of successful policy outcomes, ultimately benefiting society as a whole.

In the context of Legal Registers, engaging stakeholders is particularly crucial since Legal Registers serve as comprehensive collections of legal requirements that organizations must follow. These registers can be influenced by various stakeholders, including regulatory agencies, industry groups, and civic organizations. By involving stakeholders in the development and review of Legal Registers, organizations can ensure that they accurately reflect current legal standards and incorporate the concerns and suggestions of those impacted by these regulations. This not only enhances the relevance and applicability of the Legal Registers but also promotes a collaborative environment that reinforces adherence to legal obligations, thus minimizing risks of non-compliance. Ultimately, stakeholder influence on policy development is essential for fostering an inclusive, effective, and sustainable legal framework.

![Simple But Needed blog banner image](https://sbnsoftware.com/wp-content/uploads/SBN-Banner.png)
![Simple But Needed blog banner image](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns=%22http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%22%20viewBox=%220%200%20210%20140%22%3E%3C/svg%3E)

## Enhancing Transparency and Accountability

Enhancing transparency and accountability is a critical aspect of stakeholder engagement, especially in the context of legal registers. Legal registers refer to comprehensive records that document legal obligations, compliance requirements, and regulatory frameworks relevant to an organization. By engaging stakeholders effectively, organizations can illuminate the intricacies of these legal registers, making them more accessible and understandable to all parties involved. This fosters an environment where stakeholders can easily access and comprehend their rights and responsibilities, enhancing the overall governance structure of the organization.

The significance of transparency in legal registers cannot be overstated. When stakeholders have a clear view of the legal obligations that affect them, it boosts trust and confidence in the organization. Transparency allows stakeholders, including employees, customers, regulators, and the community, to hold the organization accountable for its actions. Without this transparency, organizations may face ambiguity and uncertainty, leading to a potential erosion of trust. Engaging stakeholders by sharing information openly about legal obligations and compliance status can mitigate risks and enhance relationships.

Accountability, on the other hand, ensures that organizations are answerable for their adherence to legal requirements. By implementing robust stakeholder engagement practices, organizations can create feedback mechanisms that allow for concerns or suggestions to be voiced and addressed promptly. Such engagement ensures that stakeholders feel valued and heard, reinforcing their commitment to the organization and its objectives. This reciprocal relationship not only aligns the organization’s operations with stakeholder expectations but also helps in identifying and rectifying potential legal and compliance issues proactively.

In essence, enhancing transparency and accountability through stakeholder engagement in legal registers is about building a foundation of trust, promoting responsible governance, and ensuring that an organization operates within the legal frameworks established. It empowers stakeholders by providing them with the information they require to engage meaningfully with the organization, paving the way for collaborative efforts toward achieving compliance and ethical conduct in business practices.

--- SOURCE 4: Buying Legal Tech: The Stakeholders to Know - Brightflag ---
URL: https://brightflag.com/resources/buying-legal-tech-the-stakeholders-to-know/

SUMMARY:
After the General Counsel, the CFO is the most important stakeholder to build a compelling business case for, as they’ll decide whether you receive the budget you need to actually make the legal tech purchase. Whether it’s members of the Sales team who might benefit from faster contract review via a CLM, or your partner in FP&A who will get quicker access to reports on legal spend, seek out other stakeholders in your organization who will benefit from the tool you want and gain their buy-in too. As with the members of your in-house team that will be using the tool, the support of these other legal tech stakeholders can impact the buying decision.

FULL CONTENT:

Buying Legal Tech: The Stakeholders to Know
===========================================

![Image 1](https://brightflag.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/04/Jake-Mendelson-150x150-1.jpg)

Jake Mendelson, Vice President, Sales at Brightflag

November 1, 2024

“Know your audience.” It’s the key to success in a variety of professional fields. But it’s also important advice for in-house legal teams that want to make a compelling case for buying the legal technology they need.

With legal spend coming under [increased scrutiny](https://www.thomsonreuters.com/en-us/posts/corporates/ldo-index-report-2024/) in recent years, there are more stakeholders involved in the legal tech buying process than ever before. That means a larger audience to appeal to when building a business case for a new software solution—as well as a wider array of interests you’ll need to tailor your business case around.

Here we’ll provide an overview of the key stakeholders that are likely to be involved in your buying process—as well the aspects of your proposed legal tech solution that will resonate with them most when building out your business case.

Legal Operations
----------------

### Role in the Buying Process

Oftentimes, it’s the [legal operations](https://brightflag.com/resources/what-is-legal-operations/) professional who kicks off the legal technology buying process. This usually occurs after a period of time spent researching the in-house legal team’s biggest pain points and/or objectives, and then cultivating a list of the legal tech solutions that would make the biggest impact.

Legal operations typically takes charge of building the business case for the technology. They garner internal support for the solution with the various internal legal technology stakeholders in order to secure the budget and necessary approvals required to make the purchase.

In organizations without a legal operations function, this work will usually be done by another member of the in-house legal team (e.g. the Assistant General Counsel, paralegal, etc.).

### What They Value

*   Operational Efficiency
*   Time Savings
*   Cost Control

Procurement
-----------

### Role in the Buying Process

Procurement’s role in the buying process is to secure the best legal tech tool at the most cost-effective price. Their responsibilities during the buying process are often dependent on whether your organization requires a [Request for Proposal (RFP)](https://brightflag.com/asset/sample-legal-e-billing-rfp/) to be created before making a purchase.

In the event an RFP is required, Procurement will usually serve as project managers of the buying process from start to finish, communicating with vendors, scheduling demos, relaying questions, etc. If there is a legal operations role in the organization, they will work in tandem with Procurement throughout this process.

If no RFP is required, Procurement typically comes in towards the end of the buying journey to negotiate pricing.

### What They Value:

*   Return on Investment
*   Cost Savings

General Counsel
---------------

### Role in the Buying Process

When it comes to buying legal tech, the General Counsel is the most important decision-maker, as no purchase can progress without their buy-in.

In some instances, the directive to find a new piece of technology for the legal department will come from the General Counsel themselves—simplifying the buying process for the member of the legal team tasked with gaining approval for a tool.

However, in most cases the General Counsel will serve as the first major stakeholder to be engaged, and will be evaluating software solutions based on their ability to [make the legal department more efficient](https://brightflag.com/resources/how-gaining-visibility-into-legal-spend-transforms-the-general-counsel-role/) and strategic.

### What They Value

*   Reducing Risk
*   Improving Legal Service Delivery
*   Data for Strategic Decision-Making

CFO
---

### Role in the Buying Process

After the General Counsel, the CFO is the most important stakeholder to build a compelling business case for, as they’ll decide whether you receive the budget you need to actually make the legal tech purchase.

As is to be expected, [the features they’ll find most compelling](https://brightflag.com/resources/cfo-legal-department-reports/) are a solution’s ability to manage costs. This can be framed in the form of reduction in spend (e.g. an e-billing tools will help the organization flag billing violations and reduce spend) or increased efficiency (e.g. easier contract review via a CLM reduces the need to add more headcount).

### What They Value:

*   Faster Revenue Generation
*   Cost Savings
*   Return on Investment (ROI)

IT
--

### Role in the Buying Process

As the technical experts, IT will be looking to see how well a tool will integrate with the organization’s current systems, and evaluating any potential security concerns. If the tool you’re considering uses [AI](https://brightflag.com/resources/legal-ai-tools/), they’ll also take the lead on reviewing common considerations surrounding the technology, including how the AI interacts with and stores legal data.

### What They Value:

*   Ease of Integration
*   Security
*   Data Compliance

Attorneys and Paralegals
------------------------

### Role in the Buying Process

Oftentimes, attorneys and paralegals are the members of the in-house team that will be interacting with the legal technology you’re looking to buy on a daily basis. Considering this, their support for a tool can be instrumental in successfully advocating for it with the other legal tech stakeholders.

They should be engaged with early and often to showcase how the tegal technology solution being considered will [improve their workflows](https://brightflag.com/resources/legal-digital-transformation/) and make their lives easier. Their positive or negative perception can ultimately impact the decision to buy.

### What They Value:

*   Time Savings
*   Improved User Experience

Other Stakeholders
------------------

### Role in the Buying Process

Legal doesn’t operate in a silo. Much of their work impacts—and is impacted by—other areas of the business. This is true when it comes to the tools they use as well.

Whether it’s members of the Sales team who might benefit from faster contract review via a CLM, or your partner in FP&A who will get quicker access to reports on legal spend, seek out other stakeholders in your organization who will benefit from the tool you want and gain their buy-in too. As with the members of your in-house team that will be using the tool, the support of these other legal tech stakeholders can impact the buying decision.

### What They Value:

*   Time Savings
*   Streamlined Workflows

Building Your Business Case for Legal Technology
------------------------------------------------

Understanding the key stakeholders involved in the buying process—and what they value—is an important first step for building an effective business case for the legal technology your in-house team needs.

If you’re looking to add an intuitive, AI-powered e-billing and matter management system to your legal tech stack, [let us know](https://brightflag.com/demo/). Here at Brightflag, we’ve helped hundreds of customers navigate the buying process. And our experienced team can serve as a trusted guide throughout the journey to help your legal department get the legal tech solutions they need to do their best work.

# Forging a Path Forward When Legal Tech Fumbles

* Legal Transformation
* 4 Mins




The use of legal technology is up 53 percent as more legal departments are searching for new ways to use these tools to manage their workflows, according to the Thomson Reuters 2023 Legal Department Operations Index. This is not surprising as innovative tools continue to enter the market and there is more eagerness across the board to derive the benefits. Smaller and medium sized organizations are joining larger organizations in the building of their [legal operations](/en-us/services/business-of-law-services) teams and tech stacks to enhance their environments with foundational tools such as contract management and matter spend management.

But what happens when a technology initiative does not live up to the expectations? This a common refrain amongst many corporate legal leaders. A lot of time and resources are spent on tech investments, so underperformance can be disheartening. It can be difficult to determine the underlying reason for the investment going off course.

It often is also tricky to determine the best path forward. Is it time to scale down the tech the department already has in-house? Would revamping current usage with new processes and efficiencies be a better option? Is it time to throw in the towel and start over with a new tool?

To answer these questions, it is first crucial to pinpoint why a tool is underperforming. Then, how to determine whether to optimize existing technology or start over with a new solution.

## Common Obstacles

When diving deeper into why a tool is not living up to expectations, it frequently becomes clear that the technology itself is not the issue.

### Here are three alternate reasons to consider when researching the root cause of a seemingly failed legal tech investment.

* The business case projections may have been too ambitious. A reassessment may show that an extended timeline or refocused use cases would lead to better results.
* The manner in which the vendor implemented the tool was not up to par with what the organization needed for it to thrive. It is important to thoroughly vet partnerships and have discussions around implementation beforehand in order to appropriately align expectations.
* The internal processes around using the new tool were weak or unclear. Change management can be hard. Even the best tools in the market can underperform without proper usage, training, and stakeholder involvement. This leads to people giving up and sticking with the old ways of completing tasks.

Considering the above will often show that the [technology](/en-us/technologies/legal-solutions) was not a failure at all. Instead, it is time to evaluate tech performance through a different lens to pinpoint valid feedback and determine where implementation or utilization fell short.

## Cultivating Successful Outcomes

There is one questions that ensues: is it time to turn back or push forward? At this point, it should be clearer how the organization is actually using current technology and it is time to set some goals for the future. Whether to optimize current tools, drop them, or invest in new tech will be the focus. Best practice is to optimize what the department already has, then look to new solutions if necessary. Optimization versus new investment costs, need for data migration, [internal compliance](/en-us/services/regulatory-compliance), and change management sentiment will all feed into this assessment.

## Three factors will guide this decision: technology roadmaps, process mapping, and stakeholder engagement.

### Technology Roadmaps

Having a functional detailed plan is crucial. Building a legal technology roadmap with clear goals in mind leads to improved operational performance management. When a tool underperforms, it is time to dive deeper. It may become clear that it is time to redefine the roadmap in order to determine the best path forward. While all roadmaps are unique and require room for change, consider including the following characteristics:

* Define a phased timeline that supports iterative development and leaves room for evolution. A major roadblock to successful implementation is attempting to accomplish too much too quickly.
* Focus on the tool’s enhancement capabilities instead of strictly looking at the functionalities.
* Consider the broader technology stack and architecture for both the legal department and entire enterprise. This will help avoid siloed operations and disjointed technology integration, while also shining light on other potential opportunities.

If these things were not done at the outset, it may be why the technology did not live up to the set expectations. Redefining the roadmap to incorporate these factors may provide an opportunity to push forward with the investment and avoid the need to be reactionary when something goes off track. Always include room to reprioritize.

## Process Mapping

Teams may avoid mapping out processes before integrating new technology because it can be time-consuming. However, failing to do so can lead to confusion over how to effectuate meaningful change and achieve uniform adoption. Process mapping will show how the department operated before the new tool came aboard and throughout the integration process. This can inform decisions about how teams can do things differently using the current technology investment, where gaps exist, and whether it is time to take a new path.

## Stakeholder Engagement

Take a look at the level of stakeholder engagement during the tech planning, implementation, and follow-up phases. These individuals need ownership and say for a tool to thrive. When key stakeholders are involved, it is easier to get the resources and training necessary for teams to effectively integrate new solutions into their workflows. A huge roadblock to effective change [management](/en-us/services/business-of-law-services/legal-spend-management) is when there is lack of stakeholder involvement and commitment to helping carry out the change.

Stakeholders should be ready, willing, and able from day one to embrace everything that goes into adopting and successfully integrating new technologies. To attain this, consider these best practices:

* Identify stakeholders and determine how much support and influence they can offer for the project. Build an initial strategy around getting the right stakeholders on board and well-positioned to effectuate meaningful change.
* Perform change impact and change fatigue assessments. These bring to light how many different areas across the enterprise that changes to technology and process will touch, how significant the impact will be, and where to proactively create mitigation strategies.
* Create a clear vision so there is a collective understanding of how to achieve success. Hallmarks of a successful plan include specific and actionable goals, considering the entire organizational architecture, and engaging pertinent stakeholders optimally at each stage of the process.
* Thoroughly communicate what each business unit needs to do before a new tool launches and schedule trainings.
* Create a continuous operational improvement plan to keep on track, measure success, receive feedback, and make revisions based on user input.

All of these steps will be more impactful with informed and engaged stakeholders taking purposeful actions to ensure a new tool thrives.

## Conclusion

Following the steps above will help legal departments better evaluate their legal tech stack and make more strategic decisions in the future. In many cases, they will likely find room to optimize – at least to some degree. There are also external [legal business advisory](/en-us/services/business-of-law-services/legal-business-advisory) resources that can help navigate tech initiatives and redefine strategies with ease. All of this will make everyone happier and create an internal culture of more informed and tech-savvy individuals.

  
*The contents of this article are intended to convey general information only and not to provide legal advice or opinions.*

Always include room to reprioritize.

## Process Mapping

Teams may avoid mapping out processes before integrating new technology because it can be time-consuming. However, failing to do so can lead to confusion over how to effectuate meaningful change and achieve uniform adoption. Process mapping will show how the department operated before the new tool came aboard and throughout the integration process. This can inform decisions about how teams can do things differently using the current technology investment, where gaps exist, and whether it is time to take a new path.

## Stakeholder Engagement

Take a look at the level of stakeholder engagement during the tech planning, implementation, and follow-up phases. These individuals need ownership and say for a tool to thrive. When key stakeholders are involved, it is easier to get the resources and training necessary for teams to effectively integrate new solutions into their workflows. A huge roadblock to effective change [management](/en-us/services/business-of-law-services/legal-spend-management) is when there is lack of stakeholder involvement and commitment to helping carry out the change.

Stakeholders should be ready, willing, and able from day one to embrace everything that goes into adopting and successfully integrating new technologies. To attain this, consider these best practices:

All of these steps will be more impactful with informed and engaged stakeholders taking purposeful actions to ensure a new tool thrives.

## Conclusion

Following the steps above will help legal departments better evaluate their legal tech stack and make more strategic decisions in the future. In many cases, they will likely find room to optimize – at least to some degree. There are also external [legal business advisory](/en-us/services/business-of-law-services/legal-business-advisory) resources that can help navigate tech initiatives and redefine strategies with ease. All of this will make everyone happier and create an internal culture of more informed and tech-savvy individuals.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Involve stakeholders from the outset to understand their needs, ensuring alignment with project goals, which fosters ownership and commitment to the technology adoption process.
Implement ongoing feedback mechanisms to capture user insights, allowing for iterative improvements and ensuring the technology meets the evolving needs of its users effectively.
Focus on intuitive design that streamlines workflows, reduces complexity, and enhances user experience, making it easier for legal professionals to adopt and utilize the technology.
Improve user satisfaction by providing tools that reduce mundane tasks, enabling legal professionals to focus on more meaningful work, thus increasing morale and productivity.
Foster a culture of innovation by encouraging users to explore new features and capabilities, promoting creative solutions that leverage technology to enhance legal service delivery.
